1.synthesis: "Simeon Peter, a servant and apostle of Jesus Christ," writes "to those who have obtained a faith of equal standing with ours by the righteousness of our God and Savior Jesus Christ" (v.1), with the prayer, "May grace and peace be multiplied to you in the knowledge of God and of Jesus our Lord" (v.2). In two verses Peter sounds the letter's keynotes: a faith equally precious for every believer, grounded in the righteousness of Christ, and growth in the "knowledge" of God and Jesus — the theme that will arm the church against the false teachers to come. And he opens with one of the New Testament's boldest confessions: Jesus Christ is "our God and Savior."
1.historical_context: 2 Peter is cast as a "testament" or farewell discourse (cf. 2 Peter 1:14), a recognized Jewish genre in which a departing leader gives final instructions, so every word carries end-of-life gravity. "A faith of equal standing" (isotimon, v.1) assures ordinary believers their faith is as valuable as the apostles'. The phrase "our God and Savior Jesus Christ" (v.1) follows the Granville Sharp construction — one article governing both nouns — that directly predicates deity of Jesus (the same construction as Titus 2:13). "Knowledge" (epignōsis, v.2) is a thematic word in the letter, set against the counterfeit teaching that threatens the church.
1.literary_structure: The salutation, like the rest of the letter, blends Jewish-Christian substance with Hellenistic philosophical vocabulary that the readers would recognize. By opening with the highest possible Christological claim — Jesus as "God and Savior" (v.1) — Peter situates the whole letter's authority and the certainty of Christ's return under the dignity of the divine Savior whom the scoffers mock (2 Peter 3:4). "Grace and peace… multiplied" (v.2) echoes the greeting of 1 Peter (1 Peter 1:2), and the emphasis on "knowledge" introduces the antidote — true knowledge of Christ — to the false teaching the letter combats.
1.theological_application: Every believer's faith is "of equal standing" with the apostles' (v.1): the humblest Christian's trust in Christ is as precious before God as Peter's own, leveling all spiritual elitism. That faith rests not on our worthiness but on "the righteousness of our God and Savior" (v.1) — Christ's righteousness, the secure ground of standing. And the way grace and peace are "multiplied" is "in the knowledge of God and of Jesus" (v.2): spiritual growth and stability come through deepening, personal knowledge of the Lord, not novel speculation.
1.christology: Peter's opening confession is unambiguous: Jesus Christ is "our God and Savior" (v.1), the full deity of the Son stated in a single article-sharing phrase. This frames the entire letter — the One whose glory Peter witnessed at the Transfiguration (2 Peter 1:16–18) and whose return the scoffers deny is none other than God himself come as Savior. "The knowledge of… Jesus our Lord" (v.2) is therefore the knowledge of God, and the saving "righteousness" by which faith is given is Christ's own.

3.synthesis: "His divine power has granted to us all things that pertain to life and godliness, through the knowledge of him who called us to his own glory and excellence" (v.3), giving "his precious and very great promises, so that through them you may become partakers of the divine nature, having escaped from the corruption that is in the world because of sinful desire" (v.4). On this basis Peter urges a chain of growth: "supplement your faith with virtue, and virtue with knowledge," then self-control, steadfastness, godliness, brotherly affection, and love (vv.5–7). These qualities keep one fruitful (v.8); their lack means blindness (v.9). "Be all the more diligent to confirm your calling and election… and there will be richly provided for you an entrance into the eternal kingdom" (vv.10–11).
3.historical_context: "Divine power" and "excellence" (aretē, v.3) reflect Hellenistic-Jewish appropriation of Greek philosophical vocabulary — aretē was the pinnacle of human flourishing in Stoic and Platonic thought — here ascribed to God himself. "Partakers of the divine nature" (v.4) borrows the language of the Greek goal of "likeness to God," but redirects it: not absorption into deity but moral participation through Christ's promises, and the "corruption" escaped is moral, the corruption of "sinful desire." The virtue-chain of vv.5–7 is a recognized Hellenistic literary form (a sorites), each virtue building toward a culminating one — here, love. "Calling and election" (v.10) are the Pauline terms for divine initiative (Romans 8:30), confirmed by the life they produce.
3.literary_structure: This passage is the positive foundation laid before the letter's polemic: a theology of God's all-sufficient provision (vv.3–4) and a ladder of Christian growth (vv.5–7) that issues in fruitfulness and final entrance into the kingdom (vv.8–11). The chain-list is bookended by faith (its base) and love (its summit), with all growth measured "in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ" (v.8). The promise of an "entrance into the eternal kingdom" (v.11) sets the eschatological hope that chapter 3 will defend against the scoffers.
3.theological_application: God has already granted "all things that pertain to life and godliness" (v.3): the Christian is not left to manufacture holiness but to draw on a provision already given in Christ. Yet this grace summons effort — "make every effort to supplement your faith" (v.5) — so that faith grows up into a whole character crowned by love (v.7). To "confirm your calling and election" (v.10) is not to earn salvation but to verify it: God's choice becomes visible and sure in a transformed life, and such a life will "never fall" but find a rich welcome into the eternal kingdom.
3.christology: The "divine power" that supplies all things for godliness is Christ's, who "called us to his own glory and excellence" (v.3) — the glory displayed at the Transfiguration (2 Peter 1:16–18). The astonishing goal, to "become partakers of the divine nature" (v.4), is what his atoning and mediating work accomplishes: the redeemed come to share, by grace and through his promises, in the very life of God. All growth is reckoned "in the knowledge of our Lord Jesus Christ" (v.8), and the kingdom entered is "the eternal kingdom of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ" (v.11).

12.synthesis: Peter explains his urgency: "I intend always to remind you of these qualities, though you know them and are established in the truth that you have" (v.12). He considers it right "to stir you up by way of reminder" as long as he is "in this body, this tent" (v.13), knowing "that the putting off of my body will be soon, as our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me" (v.14). So he will "make every effort" that after his departure they "may be able at any time to recall these things" (v.15). A dying apostle labors to leave a lasting memory of the truth.
12.historical_context: The ministry of "reminding" (v.12) reflects the Jewish pedagogical tradition of memorial rehearsal (as Moses repeatedly rehearses Sinai in Deuteronomy): foundational truths are recalled not because hearers are ignorant but because remembering is itself formative. "This body, this tent" (skēnōma, v.13) is the same body-as-temporary-dwelling image Paul uses (2 Corinthians 5:1), with its echoes of Israel's wilderness tents and the Tabernacle. Peter's foreknowledge of his death "as our Lord Jesus Christ made clear to me" (v.14) refers to Jesus' prediction of his martyrdom in John 21:18–19, linking 2 Peter to the Johannine tradition and validating his eyewitness authority.
12.literary_structure: This brief section is the formal heart of the testament genre — the departing leader's announcement of his imminent death and his concern for the community's future memory (vv.14–15). It explains the letter's whole reminding agenda and supplies the personal pathos that gives weight to the warnings ahead. The word "departure" (exodos, v.15) faintly echoes the term used of Jesus' own "exodus" discussed at the Transfiguration (Luke 9:31), the very event Peter cites next (v.16).
12.theological_application: Faithful teaching often means repeating what people already know: Peter labors to "remind" the established (v.12), because Christians do not outgrow the basics but are sustained by their constant rehearsal. His awareness that life is a temporary "tent" soon to be "put off" (vv.13–14) models a sober, fruitful relationship to mortality — using one's remaining days to serve others' future faith. And his determination that the truth outlast his death (v.15) shows the pastoral heart that thinks beyond its own lifetime to the lasting good of the church.
12.christology: Peter's calm certainty about his death rests on the word of "our Lord Jesus Christ" who "made clear to me" how it would come (v.14; cf. John 21:18–19): the risen Christ's prophecy is being fulfilled, so the apostle faces martyrdom in confident obedience to his Lord. That Jesus foreknew and foretold Peter's end confirms his sovereign authority over the apostle's life and witness, and Peter's own "departure" mirrors the path of the Christ he followed.

16.synthesis: Peter grounds the church's hope in eyewitness testimony, not invention: "we did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty" (v.16). At the Transfiguration he heard the Father's voice from the Majestic Glory: "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased" (vv.17–18). This confirms "the prophetic word," to which they do well to attend "as to a lamp shining in a dark place, until the day dawns and the morning star rises in your hearts" (v.19). For "no prophecy of Scripture comes from someone's own interpretation"; rather, "men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit" (vv.20–21).
16.historical_context: "Cleverly devised myths" (mythois, v.16) reflects the Greek philosophical distinction between mythos (illustrative fiction) and reliable logos or eyewitness report; the false teachers apparently dismissed the apostolic message as myth. Peter counters with "eyewitnesses" (epoptai, v.16) — a term used for the highest grade of initiation in the Greek mystery religions — claiming a superior, unrepeatable vision: the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1–8). The "Majestic Glory" (v.17) is a Jewish reverential title for the Shekinah, and the voice fuses the royal-Son psalm (Psalms 2:7) with the Servant (Isaiah 42:1). "Carried along" (pheromenoi, v.21) is the word for a ship driven by wind, picturing the Spirit directing the prophets without overriding their humanity.
16.literary_structure: This section defends the certainty of Christ's "coming" (v.16) on a double foundation — apostolic eyewitness of the Transfiguration (vv.16–18) and the Spirit-inspired prophetic word (vv.19–21) — directly arming the church for the assault on the Parousia in chapter 3. The dawn/morning-star imagery (v.19) draws on the messianic "star out of Jacob" (Numbers 24:17) and Scripture as a lamp in darkness (Psalms 119:105). The statement on prophecy's origin (vv.20–21) undercuts the false teachers' "twisting" of Scripture (2 Peter 3:16) and is one of the New Testament's foundational texts on inspiration.
16.theological_application: The Christian faith is anchored in history, not legend: the apostles testify to what they saw and heard (vv.16–18), so the gospel rests on eyewitness fact. Scripture is to be heeded "as to a lamp shining in a dark place" (v.19) — the believer's reliable guide through the present darkness until Christ, the morning star, fully dawns. And the doctrine of inspiration (v.21) gives Scripture its authority: because the prophets spoke "from God" as the Spirit carried them, their word is not private opinion to be twisted but God's own truth to be trusted and obeyed.
16.christology: The Transfiguration Peter witnessed (vv.16–18) was a foretaste of "the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ" (v.16): the glory then briefly unveiled is the glory in which he will return. The Father's own voice declared him "my beloved Son" (v.17), the messianic King and chosen Servant, ratifying his identity before chosen witnesses. And Christ is himself the dawning light toward which all Scripture points — "the morning star" who rises in believers' hearts (v.19), the One the Spirit-inspired prophets foretold (v.21).
